generated_comment: Honestly, in an early-stage startup every hire has to be fully available, yaar. If we start adding period leave and then 6 months maternity leave on top, the company bears the cost and the rest of the team has to stretch — aise mein it does become a fairness issue for men also. I’m not saying don’t hire women, just that founders have to think practically about project allocation and continuity.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women’s menstruation and maternity as workplace liabilities and presents accommodations as unfair special treatment compared to men. It encodes the fairness/economic-cost framing and assumes women’s biology justifies biased hiring or project decisions.
original_frame: Misogyny Jokes: Period, Hormones and Mood.
persona_text: You are Mohit. Age 27. A startup founder in Delhi. You are being interviewed on a podcast about hiring practices for early stage startups. The host asks you what your biggest hiring mistakes have been. You are giving an honest answer about a female employee you hired early on who you say brought too much personal baggage to work and why you now think carefully before hiring women who are newly married or have young children.
